report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared, this 88-year-old veteran in Franklin County who has dementia and needs help with bathing and eating, is on Medicare, and has moderate income and assets, may want to look into several Washington and federal programs. Some details are still missing (like whether Medicaid is in place, how many daily activities need help, recent hospital stays, and whether an unpaid caregiver is involved), so this screening is a starting point, not a final answer. None of this means the family qualifies for anything — only the administering agency can decide that. Please review the follow-up questions below and consider speaking with a benefits counselor or the agencies listed to get clear answers.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family member is enrolled in Medicare, lives at home rather than in a facility, and has care needs (help with bathing and eating) that suggest a possible need for skilled or personal care. This program covers part-time skilled care and aide services at home for Medicare beneficiaries, though whether the family member meets the homebound and skilled-care certification requirements is not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A and/or Part B cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, therapy services, medical social services, and home health aide care (bathing, grooming, and similar help) — but aide care is only covered while the person is also receiving skilled nursing or therapy.

To qualify, a health care provider must certify the person is homebound and needs skilled care, following a face-to-face assessment, and care must be delivered by a Medicare-certified home health agency.
Covered home health services cost $0; durable medical equipment has a 20% coinsurance after the Part B deductible.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a doctor has certified homebound status
Whether there is a documented need for skilled nursing or therapy care
nextSteps: Ask the family member's doctor about a face-to-face assessment for home health eligibility
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Franklin County

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family member is over 55, and based on the profile's income and asset ranges appears to fall within TSOA's financial limits; the dementia diagnosis and need for help with bathing and eating suggest a possible nursing-facility level of care, which is part of TSOA's functional requirement. Note that TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, so new applicants go on a waitlist rather than receiving services right away.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA offers benefits in three steps: a one-time $250 payment, up to $500 annually in Step 2, and an average of $928 per month (up to $5,568 over six months) in Step 3, covering services like respite, personal care, home-delivered meals, training, specialized equipment, and health maintenance supports.

Eligibility requires being age 55 or older, meeting nursing-facility level of care, and having income at or below $3,976/month and resources at or below $84,354 (single) as of 2026; Medicaid CN/ABP enrollment disqualifies a person from TSOA.
Program status: enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist; there is no announced reopening date.

nextSteps: Apply through washingtonconnection.org or submit form HCA 18-008 to be placed on the statewide waitlist
Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about non-Medicaid respite funds available during the TSOA pause

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family member needs help with bathing and eating, which are activities of daily living covered by this program, and is in Washington state. However, it's not yet known whether they are enrolled in community Medicaid (CN/ABP) or whether they have an unmet need in three or more ADLs, both of which affect whether this program would apply.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care assistance with ADLs such as bathing, eating, dressing, and toileting, plus related supports like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training, for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care and qualify for community Medicaid.
Eligibility requires a CARE assessment showing nursing facility level of care and financial eligibility for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the family member is enrolled in or can enroll in community Medicaid (CN/ABP)
How many activities of daily living the family member needs help with
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) through regional central intake to request a CARE assessment
Ask about current Medicaid enrollment status or apply for community Medicaid if not already enrolled

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family member has Medicare Part A, but this program only applies after a recent inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days with an ongoing need for daily skilled care, and it isn't known whether such a stay has occurred.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, following a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days.

Coverage includes a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, medical social services, medications, and supplies used in the facility; days 1-20 are free, days 21-100 have a daily coinsurance, and after day 100 the patient pays all costs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive days with an ongoing need for daily skilled care
Can you provide: recentHospitalStay?
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare will cover a skilled nursing facility stay
Keep documentation of any hospital admission dates and status (inpatient vs. observation)

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family member needs ongoing care and supervision due to dementia and help with bathing and eating, which suggests respite support could be useful for any caregiver involved. However, it is not known whether there is currently an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, which is a requirement for most of these respite programs.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite Washington vouchers offer up to $1,000 per household per year for caregivers providing 40+ hours per week of unpaid care, covering options like in-home respite and adult day services.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term care from a few hours to a few days, plus support groups, counseling, caregiver training, and equipment, tailored through a TCARE screening with a caregiver specialist; amounts vary by county.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, and how many hours per week
Can you provide: unpaidCaregiver?
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252 or waclc.org) to ask about FCSP eligibility and local respite options
If an unpaid caregiver provides 40+ hours/week of care, consider applying for a Lifespan Respite voucher

followUpQuestions: Is the family member currently enrolled in Washington Apple Health (Medicaid), and if so, what type (Categorically Needy, Alternative Benefit Plan, Medically Needy, or Medicare Savings Program)?
How many activities of daily living (such as bathing, eating, dressing, toileting, transferring) does the family member need hands-on help with?
Has there been a hospital inpatient stay of 3 or more consecutive days recently, and if so, when?
Is there an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care, and if so, about how many hours per week?
Has a doctor certified that the family member is homebound and needs skilled nursing or therapy care at home?

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at a 72-year-old veteran in Snohomish County who lives at home, needs help with bathing, dressing, and toileting, and has Medicare coverage. Based on what was shared, several Washington and federal programs may be worth a closer look, especially VA Aid & Attendance, Medicare home health, PACE, and Tailored Supports for Older Adults. A few programs need more information before it's clear whether they're a good fit, including current Medicaid enrollment status, whether there's an unpaid family caregiver, and details about hospital stays or homebound status. This is an educational screening only — none of the programs listed here have been confirmed as ones this family qualifies for. A caseworker or the administering agency for each program can make that determination.

programs.whyThisMayApply: As a veteran who served during the Gulf War era or later, with a discharge that wasn't dishonorable, needing help with daily activities like bathing and dressing, and reporting assets and income within the program's limits, several of this program's building-block criteria line up with what you've shared.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans who need help with daily activities like bathing, feeding, or dressing.
The underlying VA pension requires wartime service, a qualifying discharge, and income/net worth within limits ($163,699 net worth limit effective Dec 1, 2025).
The payment amount is based on the difference between countable income and the Maximum Annual Pension Rate (MAPR), which is higher with Aid & Attendance added.

informationStillNeeded: A completed clinical Aid & Attendance examination (VA Form 21-2680) from a medical examiner.
Documentation supporting the underlying pension application, such as service history and income/asset details.
nextSteps: Consider filing VA Form 21P-527EZ for the underlying pension.
Schedule a VA Form 21-2680 exam with a medical provider to document need for aid and attendance.
Contact a Veterans Service Organization for help with the application.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare, live at home, and have reported needing personal care help, which are starting points for this benefit. Confirming homebound status and a skilled care need (like nursing or therapy) would help clarify fit.

whatItCovers.text: Covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide care when paired with skilled care needs.
Requires homebound status, a face-to-face provider assessment, and a provider order for care from a Medicare-certified agency.
Covered services cost $0, though this benefit does not cover custodial or personal care alone if that's the only care needed.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a doctor considers you homebound (per Medicare's definition).
Whether you have a need for skilled nursing or therapy care, not just personal care help.
nextSteps: Talk to your doctor about whether you meet homebound criteria and need skilled care.
Ask your doctor for a referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency for an assessment.

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: You appear to meet the age requirement, Snohomish County is one of the four counties with PACE coverage, and your reported ADL needs align with the nursing-facility level of care standard PACE uses.

whatItCovers.text: PACE coordinates all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care plus additional services the care team determines is needed, including home care, personal care, therapy, transportation, dental, and more.
Eligibility requires being 55 or older (or 65+), living in a PACE service area, needing nursing-facility level of care, and being able to live safely in the community with PACE support.
Snohomish County is one of four Washington counties with PACE coverage, though coverage depends on specific ZIP code within the county.
Without Medicaid, PACE can still be accessed by paying a monthly premium; with Medicaid, there's no premium.

informationStillNeeded: Your specific ZIP code, to confirm it's within the PACE service area.
Financial eligibility details, since resource/income limits differ from what's already known.
nextSteps: Contact a Snohomish-area PACE provider (Providence PACE intake: 206-320-5325) to check ZIP code coverage.
Discuss functional assessment and financial eligibility steps with HCS.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You appear to meet the age (55+), income, and resource limits for TSOA, and your ADL needs suggest a nursing-facility level of care. However, TSOA is currently on a statewide enrollment pause with a waitlist, which affects timing.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA offers up to three benefit steps: a one-time $250 award, up to $500 annually, and a larger monthly benefit (averaging $928/month) for those needing a full assessment.
Covers services like caregiver assistance (respite, housekeeping, adult day care), personal assistance services, training, specialized equipment, and health maintenance supports.
Eligibility does not require Medicaid enrollment, and income/resource limits are higher than regular Medicaid — but people on CN/ABP Medicaid are not eligible.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist and no announced reopening date.

nextSteps: Apply through Washington Connection or submit form HCA 18-008 to secure a place on the waitlist by request date.
Contact your local Area Agency on Aging (1-855-567-0252) to ask about non-Medicaid respite options while waiting.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've reported needing help with bathing, dressing, and toileting — three ADLs, which lines up with the substantial-need standard this program looks for. However, whether you're enrolled in (or eligible for) community Medicaid isn't yet known, and that's a required piece for this program.

whatItCovers.text: Covers personal care assistance with ADLs (bathing, dressing, toileting, transfers, etc.) and related IADL help, plus services like relief care, nurse delegation, PERS, and caregiver training.
Requires meeting a nursing-facility level-of-care assessment and being eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in (or could enroll in) community Medicaid (CN/ABP coverage).
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) to ask about a financial application and CARE assessment.
Ask whether your Medicare-only status affects Medicaid eligibility for this program.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your veteran status, wartime service, discharge status, and net worth appear to align with this program's underlying requirements, but whether you are substantially confined to your home by a permanent disability hasn't been established, and that's the core clinical requirement for this benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Housebound benefit is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans substantially confined to their home due to a permanent disability.
It cannot be received at the same time as Aid & Attendance — a veteran receives one or the other.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are substantially confined to your home due to a permanent disability.
Details about your ADLs needing help.
Which everyday activities do they need help with?
nextSteps: Discuss with the VA or a Veterans Service Organization whether Aid & Attendance or Housebound status fits better given your situation.
If pursuing this route, arrange a VA Form 21-2680 examination documenting housebound status.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare, which is a starting requirement, but whether you've had a recent 3-day inpatient hospital stay with an ongoing skilled care need — the key trigger for this benefit — is unknown.

whatItCovers.text: Covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility after a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay, up to 100 days per benefit period.
Covers a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, medical social services, and related supplies, with cost-sharing after day 20.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you've had a recent inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days.
Can you provide: recentHospitalStay?
nextSteps: If you have an upcoming or recent hospital stay, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare will cover a SNF stay.
Keep this program in mind primarily if a hospitalization occurs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've reported needing ongoing care and supervision, which fits the general respite care need, but whether there's an unpaid family caregiver providing regular care — the central eligibility factor for these respite programs — hasn't been confirmed.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite offers vouchers up to $1,000 per household per year for respite care through contracted agencies.
The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term respite care with amounts varying by county; Snohomish County's program ranges roughly $1,000–$4,362/year on a sliding fee scale.

Lifespan Respite requires an unpaid caregiver providing 40+ hours/week of care; FCSP serves unpaid caregivers of adults 18+ who need care, with eligibility and services varying by Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you have an unpaid caregiver providing regular care, and how many hours per week they provide.

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at five programs that may be worth exploring for a 73-year-old in Walla Walla County who needs help with transferring and dressing, has dementia, is on Medicare, and has low income and modest assets. Some key facts—like whether they're already on Medicaid, exact ADL help count, whether they have an unpaid caregiver, and any recent hospital stay—are still unknown, so a few programs can't be fully assessed yet. This report explains what each program generally covers based on official program information; it does not determine eligibility. Only the administering agency can decide if someone qualifies. Please review the follow-up questions below to get a clearer picture.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is enrolled in Medicare, lives at home rather than in a facility, and has care needs related to dementia and help with transferring and dressing — all of which line up with the basic criteria for this benefit. What's not yet known is whether a doctor has certified them as homebound and whether they have a specific skilled nursing or therapy need, both of which are required.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social services, and home health aide care (only alongside skilled care) — generally at no cost for the covered services themselves.
To qualify, a person must be certified as homebound and need part-time or intermittent skilled care, based on a face-to-face provider assessment.
This benefit does not cover 24-hour care, meal delivery, or custodial/personal care alone without an accompanying skilled care need.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a doctor has certified this person as homebound
Whether this person needs skilled nursing or therapy care (not just personal care help)
nextSteps: Ask the person's doctor about a face-to-face homebound assessment and possible referral to a Medicare-certified home health agency
Ask the doctor whether a skilled care need (like therapy or nursing) exists alongside the personal care needs

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person is over 55, their income (under $1,000/month) is within the TSOA income limit, and their assets ($2,000–$85,000) fall within the single-person resource limit. Their needs with transferring and dressing may also relate to the nursing-facility level of care standard this program requires. However, note that TSOA enrollment is currently paused statewide, with new applicants going onto a waitlist.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA supports people 55+ who need nursing-facility-level care but are not on classic Medicaid, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid — up to $3,976/month income and $84,354 in resources for a single person.

It offers stepped benefits including one-time and annual funds for caregiver assistance, personal assistance services (for those without an unpaid caregiver), training, equipment, and health/therapy supports.
As of December 1, 2025, enrollment is paused statewide; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist rather than being immediately processed.

nextSteps: Apply through Washington Connection or by paper form to get on the statewide waitlist, since a place in line is established by application date

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice can cover personal care assistance with ADLs such as bathing, dressing, transferring, and toileting, plus health-related tasks, for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care and are eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid.
To continue receiving CFC after reassessment, a person generally needs an unmet or partially met need in at least three ADLs.
Services can be provided in the person's own home as well as other community settings, and clients can choose their own providers.

whatItCovers.text: COPES can provide services such as adult day care, home-delivered meals, environmental modifications, transportation, nursing services, and specialized medical equipment, layered on top of personal care services.

This benefit does not cover 24-hour care, custodial/personal care alone, or homemaker services unrelated to the care plan.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a doctor has certified this person as homebound.
Whether there is a specific skilled nursing or therapy need identified by a provider.
nextSteps: Ask the person's doctor about a face-to-face assessment for home health eligibility.
Request a list of Medicare-certified home health agencies serving Jefferson County from the provider.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person needs help with dressing, mobility, and toileting — several ADLs — which lines up with the kind of substantial daily-living needs this program is designed to help with [wa-medicaid-cfc#00-overview-eligibility]. However, whether this person is enrolled in or eligible for community Medicaid (CN/ABP) is unknown, and that is a required piece for this program.

whatItCovers.text: Community First Choice can cover personal care help with ADLs like bathing, dressing, toileting, transfers, and mobility, plus related supports like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver training, for people assessed at a nursing-facility level of care who live at home or in community settings.

To qualify functionally, a CARE assessment must find a nursing-facility level of care, and to continue services at reassessment, the person must have unmet or partially met needs in at least three ADLs.
Financially, the person generally must be eligible for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) rather than institutional Medicaid rules.

informationStillNeeded: Whether this person is enrolled in, or can enroll in, community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) coverage.
nextSteps: Contact regional Home and Community Services (HCS) to request a CARE functional assessment.
Ask HCS whether current income and assets could support eligibility for community Medicaid CN/ABP coverage, since this determination must come from the agency.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person has Medicare Part A, but whether there has been a recent 3-day inpatient hospital stay with a daily skilled-care need is unknown — and that is a required condition for this benefit, so it's unclear yet whether this program fits the current situation [medicare-snf#00-overview-eligibility].

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover short-term skilled nursing facility care — including skilled nursing, therapy, meals, and medical supplies — for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 days in a row.
This is not a long-term custodial care benefit, and days 21-100 involve daily coinsurance costs.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there has been a recent inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days.
Whether a doctor has determined a need for daily skilled nursing or therapy care.
Can you provide: recentHospitalStay?
nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether it qualifies as a 3-day inpatient stay for SNF coverage purposes.
Ask the doctor whether skilled nursing facility care might be appropriate given the stroke history.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This person needs ongoing help with several daily activities due to a stroke, which fits the kind of ongoing care need these respite programs are meant to support [wa-respite-care#00-overview-eligibility]. However, it's not yet known whether an unpaid family member or friend is providing regular care, which is a key requirement for most respite funding.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite vouchers can provide up to $1,000 per household per year to unpaid caregivers providing 40+ hours per week of care, used toward in-home respite, adult day services, or other approved providers.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term relief care (in-home, adult day center, or residential facility) plus caregiver support groups, training, and consultation, with amounts varying by local Area Agency on Aging.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there is an unpaid caregiver (such as a family member) regularly providing care or supervision, and how many hours per week.
Can you provide: unpaidCaregiver?
nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) to ask about respite options available in Jefferson County.
If a family caregiver is involved, ask about a TCARE screening through the local Area Agency on Aging.

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: The applicant is 90 (well above the 55+ threshold), lives in Snohomish County, which is one of PACE's four covered counties in Washington, and has care needs (Parkinson's, help with bathing and toileting) that could support a nursing-facility-level-of-care finding.

whatItCovers.text: PACE coordinates all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care through an interdisciplinary team, including home care, personal care, medical specialty services, prescription drugs, transportation, and more, for people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community.

Eligibility requires being 55 or older, living in a PACE service area, needing nursing-home level of care as certified by the state, and being able to live safely in the community with PACE's help; Snohomish County has 21 ZIP codes covered, though not every ZIP in the county is included.

informationStillNeeded: The applicant's specific ZIP code, to confirm it falls within a PACE-covered area of Snohomish County.
nextSteps: Contact a Washington PACE organization directly — Providence PACE at (206) 320-5325 covers an Everett South center that may serve Snohomish County — to confirm ZIP code coverage.
Ask about the state's nursing-facility-level-of-care certification process alongside a PACE inquiry.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The applicant is 90 (well above the 55+ age threshold), and the profile indicates income and assets that would likely fall within TSOA's generous limits ($3,976/month income cap and $84,354/$156,883 resource caps), along with care needs suggesting a possible nursing-facility-level-of-care finding. Note that TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants going onto a waitlist.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA offers benefits in three steps — a one-time $250 amount, up to $500/year for caregiver or personal assistance services, and roughly $928/month (capped at $5,568 over six months) for those needing the highest tier — covering things like respite, personal care, home-delivered meals, and specialized equipment.

TSOA does not require Medicaid enrollment and serves people 55+ needing nursing-facility level of care, with income and resource limits well above regular Medicaid; however, enrollment has been paused since December 1, 2025, and new applications currently only join a statewide waitlist rather than receiving immediate services.

nextSteps: Contact Community Living Connections (1-855-567-0252) or apply via washingtonconnection.org to be placed on the statewide TSOA waitlist.
Ask the local Area Agency on Aging about non-Medicaid respite funding options (FCSP) while waiting, since TSOA intake is currently paused.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The applicant has Medicare Part A, which is a basic requirement for this benefit. However, whether there has been a recent 3-day inpatient hospital stay with a daily skilled-care need is unknown, and this is central to whether the benefit would apply.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans who need help with daily activities like bathing, feeding, or dressing.

The underlying pension requires wartime service (including the Vietnam era, 8/5/1964–5/7/1975), a discharge that was not dishonorable, and net worth within limits ($163,699 effective December 1, 2025).

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers short-term skilled nursing and rehabilitation care in a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days.
Covered services include a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing care, therapy services, and medical social services, with cost-sharing starting on day 21 of a benefit period.

programs.whyThisMayApply: We don't yet know this person's age, care needs, income, or assets, so it's not possible to say whether COPES may be worth looking into. COPES is generally for adults 65+ or with a qualifying disability who need nursing-facility-level care and meet Medicaid income/asset limits.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid waiver that layers additional home-and-community-based services on top of Community First Choice, including adult day care, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, and transportation, for people living in their own home, an adult family home, or assisted living.

To be considered, a person generally must be 65+ or 18+ with a qualifying disability, need nursing-facility level of care as determined by a CARE assessment, and meet income and asset limits (income up to $2,982/month in 2026, assets at or below $2,000 for a single person, with special rules for couples).

whatItCovers.text: CFC is a Medicaid state plan option providing personal care assistance with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, and mobility, plus services like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver training, for people who meet an institutional level of care but live in the community.

Eligibility generally requires being 18+, having a CARE assessment show a nursing-facility level of care need, and being financially eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC covers nursing facility care and serves as the financial gateway to HCS waivers (like COPES), PACE, hospice in an institution, and community LTSS such as CFC.

Eligibility generally requires meeting Apple Health general requirements, an institutional or waiver-based status, a nursing-facility level of care determination, and income/resource limits (income up to $2,982/month or the Medically Needy pathway, resources generally $2,000 for a single person).

To use the benefit, a worker generally needs to have met contribution requirements (such as 10 years of work, or 3 of the last 6 years) and need help with 3 or more activities of daily living expected to last at least 90 days.

Eligibility generally requires meeting underlying VA pension requirements (non-dishonorable discharge, wartime service, and income/net worth limits, currently $163,699), plus a clinical need for help with daily activities.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared, this 87-year-old family member in Clallam County has dementia, lives in a nursing facility, is on Medicare, and has low income and assets — facts that make a few Washington programs worth a closer look. However, some key details (like how much help is needed with daily activities, and whether there was a recent 3-day hospital stay) are still unknown, so this screening can only point you toward programs to ask about, not confirm anything. Please treat every item below as a starting point for a conversation with the actual agency, not a decision.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is 87 (meeting the 65+ age rule), has monthly income under $1,000 and assets under $2,000 — both within the limits described for this program — and has a dementia diagnosis, which is one of the factors considered under the program's nursing-facility-level-of-care criteria. However, whether the nursing-facility level of care requirement is met, and details about current living situation, are not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds services on top of Medicaid personal care, including adult day care/health, wellness education, environmental modifications, home-delivered meals, nursing services, specialized medical equipment, and transportation.

Eligibility generally requires being 65+ (or having an SSI-criteria disability), meeting nursing-facility level of care (which can include cognitive impairment like disorientation or memory impairment plus an unmet ADL need), income at or below $2,982/month, and resources at or below $2,000 for a single person.
Application involves a financial application (HCA 18-005) plus a CARE assessment requested through the regional HCS intake line.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a nursing-facility level of care assessment (CARE assessment) has been completed and what it found
Current living situation details, since this affects how COPES services would apply
Which everyday activities do they need help with?
Where do they currently live?
nextSteps: Contact the regional HCS central intake line to request a CARE assessment.
Submit the HCA 18-005 financial application through washingtonconnection.org or by mail/fax to HCS-LTSS.
Ask a DSHS or Area Agency on Aging caseworker how COPES relates to a nursing facility placement, since COPES is generally for in-home, adult family home, or assisted living settings.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is 87, is currently residing in a nursing facility, is covered by Medicare, and has income and assets within the limits described for this program (under $1,000/month income and under $2,000 in assets). Residing in a medical institution is a specific pathway to eligibility mentioned in the program materials, though whether the 30-consecutive-day institutional stay threshold and nursing-facility level of care requirement have been formally established is not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: This program is Washington's Medicaid coverage for long-term services and supports, including nursing facility care, and is the financial gateway to waivers like COPES, PACE, and other community LTSS options.
Institutional status can be met by residing in a medical institution for 30 consecutive days or more, or being assessed as likely to do so.

Covers nursing facility care including 24-hour supervised nursing care, personal care, therapy, nutrition management, organized activities, social services, room, board, and laundry, plus full Apple Health medical benefits.
Income eligibility follows the Special Income Level ($2,982/month) or a Medically Needy pathway; resource limit is $2,000 for a single person, with a home equity limit of $1,130,000.

Application can be made online at washingtonconnection.org, by paper form HCA 18-005, by phone, or in person, followed by a financial interview and a CARE assessment by an HCS social worker; processing standard is 45 days (60 if a disability decision is required).

informationStillNeeded: Whether the nursing-facility level of care requirement (via CARE assessment) has been met
How long your family member has resided in, or is expected to reside in, the nursing facility
Which everyday activities do they need help with?
Where do they currently live?
nextSteps: Start the HCA 18-005 application through washingtonconnection.org, by mail/fax, or at an HCS office.
Call regional HCS central intake (for Clallam County, this falls under Region 3: 800-786-3799) to arrange a CARE assessment.
Gather documentation: proof of income, resource statements, Medicare information, and any transfer/gift records from the past 5 years.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your family member is enrolled in Medicare, which is one requirement for this coverage. However, it's not yet known whether there was a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay immediately before the nursing facility admission, which is required for Medicare to cover the stay — this program covers short-term skilled care, not indefinite custodial care, so it may or may not be the current basis for the facility stay.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A can cover up to 100 days per benefit period of skilled nursing facility care after a qualifying hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive inpatient days, including semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapies, medical social services, medications, and supplies used in the facility.
Days 1–20 have no daily cost; days 21–100 have a coinsurance of $217/day (2026); after day 100, the patient pays all costs.
A doctor's determination that daily skilled care is needed is also required, and the SNF must be Medicare-certified.

informationStillNeeded: Whether there was a hospital stay of 3 or more consecutive inpatient days right before the current nursing facility stay
Whether a doctor has certified an ongoing need for daily skilled nursing or therapy care

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at a family profile for an 86-year-old widowed adult in Grays Harbor County living with a family member, who has dementia and needs help with transferring and eating, is covered by Medicare, and has countable assets under $2,000. Several Washington programs and Medicare benefits appear worth a closer look based on age, care needs, and asset level. However, some key pieces of information—especially monthly income, whether the person is already enrolled in community Medicaid, homebound status, recent hospital stays, and whether an unpaid caregiver is involved—are still unknown. Because income and a few other details are missing for multiple programs, this screening should be treated as a starting point, not a determination of eligibility. A conversation with the listed agencies is the best next step to confirm what actually applies.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare home health can cover part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational therapy, medical social services, and home health aide care (help with walking, bathing, feeding) when also receiving skilled nursing or therapy — at $0 cost for covered services.

To qualify, a person generally needs a certified homebound status, a face-to-face assessment by a provider, and care ordered by a provider and delivered through a Medicare-certified home health agency.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a doctor has certified the person as homebound.
Whether the person has a documented need for skilled nursing or therapy care.

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at your family's situation — a 70-year-old living alone in Douglas County, Washington, needing help with dressing, on Medicare, with modest income and savings — against five programs. A few things are still unknown, especially whether you're enrolled in (or eligible for) Washington's community Medicaid, how many daily activities you need help with, whether you've had a recent 3-day hospital stay, and whether anyone is providing you unpaid care. Because several of these key details are missing, this report leans heavily on follow-up questions rather than firm recommendations. None of this is an eligibility decision — only the administering agency can determine that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You're enrolled in Medicare and have an indicated care need (help with dressing), and you live at home rather than in a facility — all of which line up with this benefit's basic requirements. What's not yet known is whether a doctor considers you homebound and whether you need skilled nursing or therapy care, which are required for this benefit.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare covers part-time or intermittent skilled nursing care, physical/occupational/speech therapy, medical social services, and home health aide care (help with bathing, walking, and grooming) — but aide care is only covered when skilled nursing or therapy is also being provided.

To qualify, a person must need part-time or intermittent skilled care, be considered homebound, have a face-to-face provider assessment, and receive care ordered by a provider through a Medicare-certified agency.
Covered home health services cost $0; custodial or personal care alone, without a skilled care need, is not covered.

informationStillNeeded: Whether a doctor has certified you as homebound
Whether you have a documented need for skilled nursing or therapy care
nextSteps: Ask your doctor whether you meet the homebound and skilled-care criteria for a home health referral.
If eligible, your doctor can refer you to a Medicare-certified home health agency for a face-to-face assessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You are 70, live in your own home in Dourouglas County, need help with dressing, and have income under $1,000/month with assets between $2,000 and $85,000 — figures that fall within typical Medicaid-related ranges. However, whether you're enrolled in or eligible for community Medicaid (CN/ABP), and whether you need help with 3 or more ADLs, are both unknown, so this program's applicability can't yet be assessed.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care assistance with ADLs including dressing, bathing, eating, and toileting, plus IADLs like meal prep and housework, for people who meet a nursing-facility level of care and are eligible for community Medicaid.
It also covers relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and caregiver management training.

Eligibility requires meeting an institutional level of care and having at least 3 ADLs with unmet or partially met needs at reassessment, along with financial eligibility for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are enrolled in, or able to enroll in, community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan)
How many activities of daily living you need substantial help with
nextSteps: Contact Home and Community Services (HCS) to request a CARE functional assessment.
Ask about your Medicaid enrollment status or apply for community Medicaid if not already enrolled.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You have Medicare Part A, which is a baseline requirement for this benefit. However, this program only applies after a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay with a resulting need for daily skilled care, and it's unknown whether you've had such a stay. Based on your profile (living at home, needing help with dressing), this benefit may not currently be relevant unless a hospitalization occurs.

whatItCovers.text: Medicare Part A covers short-term skilled nursing and rehab care in a certified skilled nursing facility for up to 100 days per benefit period, but only after a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days.

Covered services include a semi-private room, meals, skilled nursing, therapy services, medical social services, and medications/supplies used in the facility; days 1-20 cost $0, days 21-100 have a daily coinsurance, and days beyond 100 are the patient's full responsibility.

nextSteps: If a hospital stay occurs, ask hospital discharge staff whether Medicare will cover a follow-up SNF stay before admission.

programs.whyThisMayApply: At 70, your age meets TSOA's 55+ requirement, and your reported income and assets appear to fall within TSOA's financial limits. However, whether you meet the nursing-facility level of care requirement is unknown, and importantly, TSOA enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a waitlist rather than processed immediately.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA offers tiered benefits for older adults needing nursing-facility level care who are not on classic Medicaid, including a one-time $250 benefit, an annual $500 benefit, and a larger ongoing monthly benefit (recently around $928/month) for caregiver support or personal assistance services.

Eligibility requires being age 55+, meeting nursing-facility level of care, and having income/resources within specified limits ($3,976/month income; $84,354 in resources for a single person as of 1/1/2026), and not being enrolled in CN/ABP Medicaid.

Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist and no announced reopening date; people already enrolled keep their services.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite vouchers offer up to $1,000 per household per year for caregivers providing 40+ hours/week of unpaid care, delivered through contracted respite agencies statewide.

The Family Caregiver Support Program (FCSP) offers planned short-term respite care (in-home, adult day center, or residential facility) plus caregiver support services, with amounts varying by county's Area Agency on Aging.

report.overallSummary: We don't yet have enough information about this family's specific care needs, age, income, and daily living help to say which programs are worth pursuing. Based on what was shared — an adult in Okanogan County who owns their home and has countable assets between $2,000 and $85,000 — several Washington long-term care and caregiver-support programs might be worth a closer look, but almost every key eligibility factor (age, health/ADL needs, income, Medicaid or Medicare enrollment, and caregiver situation) is still unknown. This report highlights what each program generally covers and what questions would need to be answered before a professional could say more. Please note that two of the programs (TSOA and MAC) currently have paused enrollment statewide with a waitlist in effect.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The family lives in Okanogan County, Washington, and reports countable assets between $2,000 and $85,000, which overlaps with the $2,000 single resource limit this program uses, but we don't yet know the person's age, income, or level of daily care need, all of which are required to assess this waiver.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid waiver operated by DSHS that adds services like adult day care, home-delivered meals, skilled nursing, environmental modifications, and transportation on top of Community First Choice personal care, for people in their own home, an adult family home, or assisted living.

To be considered, a person generally must be 65+ or 18+ with an SSI-criteria disability, be assessed as needing nursing-facility level of care, have income at or below the Special Income Level ($2,982/month as of 1/1/2026), and have countable resources at or below $2,000 (single).

To be considered, a person generally needs to be eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community, and be assessed as needing nursing-facility level of care, with at least three ADLs needing help to continue services at reassessment.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This is the main financial gateway to several Washington long-term care Medicaid programs. Given the family's countable assets fall in a range that could be near Medicaid limits, this may be worth investigating, though age, income, and care-need details are still unknown.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC covers nursing facility care for people needing that level of care, and serves as the financial gateway to HCS waivers like COPES, PACE, hospice in an institution, and community LTSS programs such as CFC.

Eligibility generally requires meeting general Apple Health requirements, an institutional status or waiver-equivalent status, a nursing-facility level of care assessment, and either SSI-related or MAGI-based financial criteria, with income tests including the Special Income Level ($2,982/month) or the Medically Needy pathway.
For SSI-related eligibility, resource limits are $2,000 single/$3,000 couple, with home equity capped at $1,130,000 and spousal impoverishment protections available.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA offers benefits in three steps: a one-time $250 payment, up to $500/year for caregiver-related services, and roughly $928/month (up to $5,568 over six months) for more extensive support, covering caregiver assistance, personal assistance, training, equipment, and health/therapy supports.

Eligibility generally requires the care receiver to be 55+, not enrolled in Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid, assessed as needing nursing-facility level of care, and within TSOA's income ($3,976/month) and resource ($84,354 single) limits.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with new applicants placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist and no announced reopening date.

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at nine programs that may relate to a 71-year-old in Washington state with dementia who needs help bathing, transferring, and moving around, and who is covered by Medicare. Several programs — including Medicaid-based long-term care options, PACE, and Medicare's home health and skilled nursing benefits — appear worth a closer look based on age and care needs alone. However, key facts like income, assets, marital status, county, current Medicaid enrollment, veteran status, and living situation are still unknown. Because so much financial and situational information is missing, this report leans heavily on follow-up questions rather than firm recommendations. Nothing here is an eligibility determination — only the actual administering agency can confirm eligibility.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is 71 (age 65+) and has care needs (bathing, transferring, mobility issues, dementia) that may meet the nursing-facility level-of-care standard COPES requires, since the program looks at ADL needs and cognitive impairment with unmet ADL need [wa-medicaid-copes#01-overview-eligibility-part-2].

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid waiver that layers services like adult day care, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, and transportation on top of personal care, for people living at home, in adult family homes, or assisted living.

Eligibility generally requires being age 65+ (or having an SSI-criteria disability) and meeting a nursing-facility level of care standard, which can include cognitive impairment (such as dementia-related disorientation or wandering) combined with an unmet ADL need.

There are also income and asset limits — income at or below about $2,982/month (or a lower medically-needy level) and resources at or below $2,000 for a single person, with special rules for married couples.

informationStillNeeded: Monthly income amount
Countable assets/resources amount
Marital status
Whether the person is enrolled in Medicaid
nextSteps: Contact the regional Home and Community Services (HCS) intake line to request a CARE assessment.
Complete the financial application (HCA 18-005) through washingtonconnection.org or by mail.
Discuss income and asset details with an HCS financial specialist to see which pathway may apply.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person's needs with bathing, transferring, and mobility suggest a substantial ADL need, which is one of the factors CFC considers for ongoing services [wa-medicaid-cfc#00-overview-eligibility].

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with ADLs like bathing, transferring, dressing, and mobility, plus services such as relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training, in settings including the person's own home or assisted living.

To qualify, a person generally needs a CARE assessment showing a nursing-facility level of care, and must be eligible for community Medicaid (Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan) or SSI-related coverage.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is currently enrolled in or could enroll in community Medicaid (CN/ABP)
nextSteps: Ask HCS about current or potential Medicaid enrollment status.
Request a CARE functional assessment through HCS central intake.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person's age (71) and care needs may fit the age and functional criteria for Apple Health long-term care coverage, which serves as a gateway to several LTSS programs [wa-apple-health-ltc#00-overview-eligibility-part-1].

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC covers nursing facility care for those meeting a nursing-facility level of care and serves as the financial gateway to HCS waivers like COPES, PACE, and community LTSS programs such as CFC.
Eligibility depends on meeting SSI-related or MAGI-based financial criteria, with an income limit around $2,982/month or a medically-needy pathway, and resource limits of $2,000 for a single person.

It requires meeting underlying VA pension rules, including wartime service, discharge status, and net worth/income limits, plus one A&A clinical criterion.

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at your situation — living in your own home in Washington, needing help with eating, bathing, and toileting, a Parkinson's diagnosis, and monthly income between $1,000 and $2,999 — against several programs that may help pay for or provide care. Several key facts we don't yet have (your age, veteran status, county, Medicaid/Medicare enrollment, countable assets, marital status, and whether you have an unpaid caregiver) mean we can't yet tell which programs are truly worth pursuing versus which are unlikely to fit. This report should be treated as a starting point for questions to ask a caseworker or benefits counselor, not as a determination of eligibility — only the administering agency can decide that. Please answer the follow-up questions below so a more useful, focused screening can be done.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've told us you need help with eating, bathing, and toileting, which are activities of daily living that this program's assessment considers — the rules engine found this passes as a possible nursing-facility-level-of-care indicator (wa-medicaid-copes#01-overview-eligibility-part-2). However, your age, income bracket detail, assets, marital status, and Medicaid enrollment status are unknown, so we can't tell whether the financial and age/disability criteria would likely be met.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds services on top of Community First Choice personal care, including adult day care/health, home-delivered meals, nursing services, specialized equipment, transportation, and environmental modifications, available in a private residence, adult family home, or assisted living facility.

Eligibility generally requires being 18+ with an SSI-criteria disability or 65+, meeting nursing-facility level of care, and financial limits including a Special Income Level of $2,982/month and a resource limit of $2,000 for a single person (as of 1/1/2026).

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care assistance with ADLs and IADLs, relief care, nurse delegation, personal emergency response systems, assistive technology, and caregiver management training, available in your own home or other community settings.

whatItCovers.text: WA Cares offers a lifetime benefit of $36,500 (2026) that pays approved providers directly for services like in-home personal care, home-delivered meals, home modifications, adult day services, professional nursing, and respite care.

To use the benefit, a person generally must have contributed through payroll premiums for a qualifying period (10 years without a long interruption, or 3 of the past 6 years, or qualify under the near-retiree transition rule) and need help with 3 or more ADLs expected to last at least 90 days.

whatItCovers.text: Aid & Attendance is a monthly cash payment added to a VA pension for veterans (or some survivors) who need help with daily activities, calculated as the difference between countable income and a maximum annual pension rate.

To qualify for the underlying pension, a person generally needs a discharge that was not dishonorable, net worth within VA limits ($163,699 effective December 1, 2025), and qualifying wartime service.

report.overallSummary: We don't yet have enough information about this family to say which programs are worth pursuing with confidence. We know the person is 74 years old, lives in Washington state, has a Parkinson's diagnosis, and is widowed. We don't know their income, assets, county, insurance coverage (Medicare or Medicaid), living situation, how much help they need with daily activities, or whether an unpaid caregiver is involved. Because so many key facts are unknown, this screening should be treated as a starting point for questions to ask, not a set of recommendations. Please review the follow-up questions below and consider reaching out to Washington's Aging and Disability Resource network (Community Living Connections, 1-855-567-0252) to talk through options with a professional.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person is 74, which meets the age 65+ pathway for this waiver (wa-medicaid-copes#01-overview-eligibility-part-2). However, whether the Parkinson's diagnosis translates into a level of daily-activity need that meets the program's functional test, and whether income and assets fit the limits, are not yet known from the profile.

whatItCovers.text: COPES adds services like adult day care, home-delivered meals, skilled nursing, environmental modifications, and transportation on top of personal care, for people living at home, in an adult family home, or assisted living.

To be eligible, a person generally must be 65+ (or 18+ with a qualifying disability), be assessed as needing nursing-facility-level care, and meet income and asset limits (for 2026, income up to $2,982/month and assets up to $2,000 for a single person, with some exceptions).

informationStillNeeded: How much help is needed with daily activities like bathing, eating, or moving (this determines the level-of-care requirement)
Current living situation (own home, adult family home, assisted living)
Approximate monthly income
Approximate countable assets/resources
Marital status for benefit calculation purposes
Whether currently enrolled in Medicaid/Apple Health
Where do they currently live?
nextSteps: Contact the local HCS office or call the regional central intake line to request a CARE assessment.
Gather income and asset documentation in case a financial application (HCA 18-005) is needed.
Discuss with a DSHS or Area Agency on Aging counselor whether this waiver fits the specific care needs.

programs.whyThisMayApply: CFC serves adults with substantial help needed in several daily activities who have (or can get) community Medicaid coverage. The profile doesn't yet indicate Medicaid enrollment or how many daily activities require help, so this is only a possible match to explore.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care help with bathing, dressing, eating, mobility, and similar tasks, plus services like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver training, in home or community settings.

To qualify, a person generally needs to already have (or be able to get) Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage, and be assessed as needing help with three or more daily activities to continue receiving CFC.

informationStillNeeded: Whether the person is currently enrolled in Apple Health/Medicaid (CN or ABP)
How many daily activities require hands-on help
nextSteps: Ask HCS about a CARE functional assessment.
Check current Apple Health enrollment status through Washington Connection.

programs.whyThisMayApply: This is the general Medicaid gateway to long-term care services, and age 65+ meets one basic criterion (wa-apple-health-ltc#00-overview-eligibility-part-1). We don't yet know income, assets, or the level of daily care needed, all of which determine whether this pathway is worth pursuing.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC covers nursing facility care and serves as the financial gateway to other home and community based programs like COPES, PACE, and CFC.

Eligibility generally requires meeting nursing-facility level of care and fitting within income limits (around $2,982/month in 2026) and asset limits (around $2,000 for a single person), with a Medically Needy pathway available for higher incomes.

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: Age 74 meets PACE's basic age threshold, but PACE is only available in four Washington counties (King, Pierce, Spokane, Snohomish), and we don't know which county this family lives in or the level of daily care needed.

whatItCovers.text: PACE provides all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care plus additional services like adult day primary care, home care, personal care, therapy, and transportation, coordinated by a care team, for people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community.
PACE is only available to people living in the service area of a Washington PACE organization, which as of the most recent retrieval covers ZIP codes in King, Pierce, Spokane, and Snohomish counties.

informationStillNeeded: County and ZIP code of residence
Level of daily-activity help needed
Which Washington county do they live in?
Which everyday activities do they need help with?
nextSteps: Check whether the home address falls within a PACE service area by contacting a local PACE organization intake line.
Ask about the state's nursing-facility level of care certification process.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The person's age (74) meets TSOA's age threshold, but this program is currently under an enrollment pause statewide, and we don't know income, assets, Medicaid status, or care needs to assess further relevance.

whatItCovers.text: TSOA offers stepped benefits (up to about $928/month, or up to $5,568 over six months at Step 3) for caregiver support and personal assistance services for people 55+ needing nursing-facility-level care who are not on regular Medicaid.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025; new applicants are placed on a first-come, first-served waitlist with no announced reopening date.

programs.whyThisMayApply: MAC requires that the care receiver already be on Apple Health CN/ABP Medicaid and have an unpaid caregiver, both of which are unknown here, and the program is also currently paused statewide.

whatItCovers.text: MAC provides stepped caregiver support benefits (similar structure to TSOA) for unpaid caregivers of Medicaid-enrolled people 55+ who meet nursing-facility level of care but have chosen not to use other Medicaid LTSS.
Enrollment has been paused statewide since December 1, 2025, with a first-come, first-served waitlist, the same pause affecting TSOA.

whatItCovers.text: Lifespan Respite vouchers offer up to $1,000/year for caregivers providing 40+ hours/week of unpaid care, and the Family Caregiver Support Program offers planned short-term relief care and support services, with amounts and rules varying by local Area Agency on Aging.

report.overallSummary: Based on what you've shared — needing help with mobility, transferring, and dressing, a dementia diagnosis, monthly income under $1,000, and living in Washington state — several programs may be worth a closer look. However, many key details are still unknown (your age, veteran status, county, current Medicaid/Medicare enrollment, savings/assets, and whether you have an unpaid family caregiver). Because so much information is missing, this screening should be treated as a starting point for questions to ask an official agency, not a determination of what you'll receive. No program listed here can be confirmed as something you qualify for — only the administering agency can decide that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: You've described needing help with mobility, transferring, and dressing, along with a dementia diagnosis and income under $1,000/month — these facts align with COPES's focus on people needing nursing-facility level of care with income under its Special Income Level of $2,982/month (wa-medicaid-copes#01-overview-eligibility-part-2). However, your age, asset level, and Medicaid enrollment status are unknown, and those are required pieces of the eligibility picture.

whatItCovers.text: COPES layers services on top of Community First Choice personal care, including adult day care/health, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, transportation, and help with transition costs like rental deposits.

Eligibility generally requires being 65+, or 18-64 with an SSI-criteria disability, needing nursing-facility level of care (which can include cognitive impairment plus ADL needs), and having income/assets within Medicaid limits.

informationStillNeeded: Your age
Whether you have a disability determination under SSI criteria (if under 65)
Your countable assets/savings
Your marital status
Whether you are currently enrolled in Medicaid
How old is the person who needs care?
nextSteps: Contact your regional HCS office to request a CARE assessment.
Complete the Washington Apple Health financial application (HCA 18-005) to begin the eligibility process.
Ask an HCS caseworker how your assets and income would be evaluated under COPES rules.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your described needs with dressing, transferring, and mobility suggest substantial help with activities of daily living, which is central to CFC's personal care services (wa-medicaid-cfc#00-overview-eligibility). Whether you're already enrolled in community Medicaid is unknown, which is a key missing piece.

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care assistance with ADLs like bathing, dressing, transferring, and mobility, plus services like relief care, personal emergency response systems, and caregiver management training.

To qualify, someone generally needs to already be eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community, along with a functional assessment showing nursing-facility level of care.

informationStillNeeded: Whether you are currently enrolled in, or could enroll in, community Medicaid (CN/ABP)
nextSteps: Ask HCS about your current or potential Medicaid enrollment status.
Request a CARE functional assessment through HCS central intake.

programs.whyThisMayApply: Your described care needs and income under $1,000/month fall within the income threshold this program considers (wa-apple-health-ltc#00-overview-eligibility-part-1), and this program is the financial gateway to several of the other services listed here. Your age and asset information are unknown, so a full picture isn't yet possible.

whatItCovers.text: Apple Health LTC covers nursing facility care and serves as the financial gateway to community-based waiver programs like COPES, PACE, and community LTSS such as CFC.

Eligibility generally depends on meeting SSI-related or MAGI-based criteria, income at or below the Special Income Level ($2,982/month), and resources within the $2,000 single limit (with some exceptions).

However, most of the key details that determine whether these programs might actually apply (such as age, specific care needs, veteran status, county of residence, and current insurance coverage) are still unknown. Because so much information is missing, this screening cannot yet point to strong matches. It's most useful right now as a guide for what questions to explore next, rather than a set of recommendations. Please note that none of these programs can be confirmed as options until the family answers more questions and connects with the actual agency running each program — eligibility can only be determined by that agency, not by this screening.

report.overallSummary: This screening looked at a 91-year-old living at home in Washington who needs help with dressing, mobility, and eating, has heart disease, low monthly income, and modest assets. Several Washington and federal programs that help pay for in-home care and personal care appear worth a closer look, but some key details — like exact countable assets, marital status, current Medicaid/Medicare enrollment, county of residence, and whether there's an unpaid family caregiver — are still unknown. Because these facts are missing, this report leans on follow-up questions rather than firm recommendations. None of this is an eligibility decision; only the administering agency can determine that.

programs.whyThisMayApply: The profile describes a 91-year-old (meeting the age 65+ rule) who needs help with dressing, mobility, and eating — needs that align with the nursing-facility-level-of-care criteria this waiver uses — and reports monthly income under $1,000, which is within the program's income limits. However, the exact countable asset amount and marital status are not yet known, and both affect whether the resource limit is met.

whatItCovers.text: COPES is a Medicaid waiver serving people in their own homes, adult family homes, or assisted living, and covers services like adult day care, home-delivered meals, nursing services, environmental modifications, and transportation, layered on top of personal care.

Eligibility generally requires being 65+ (or having an SSI-criteria disability), needing nursing-facility level of care based on an assessment, having income within set limits, and having countable resources at or below $2,000 for a single person (with higher limits for married couples).

whatItCovers.text: CFC covers personal care assistance with ADLs such as bathing, dressing, eating, and mobility, along with services like personal emergency response systems and caregiver management training, for people in their own home or other community settings.

Financial eligibility requires being eligible for Categorically Needy or Alternative Benefit Plan Medicaid coverage in the community, and functional eligibility requires an assessment finding nursing-facility level of care.

programs.programName: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.whyThisMayApply: Age 91 meets the age requirement, and the described ADL needs align with the nursing-facility level of care criterion. However, PACE is only available in certain counties, and the person's county of residence is not yet known.

whatItCovers.text: PACE provides all Medicare- and Medicaid-covered care coordinated by an interdisciplinary team, including home care, personal care services, nursing home care, and transportation, for people who need nursing-home-level care but can live safely in the community.

Eligibility requires being 55 or older, living in a PACE service area, needing nursing-home level of care, and being able to live safely in the community with PACE support; Washington PACE service areas currently cover only King, Pierce, Spokane, and Snohomish counties.
